title: Abstract
content: KNb 3 O 8 and HNb 3 O 8 nanobelts were prepared by hydrothermal synthesis.The characteristics of samples were investigated by XRD, SEM, and UV-vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy.The KNb 3 O 8 and HNb 3 O 8 nanobelts exhibited much higher activities for CO 2 photoreduction to methane than commercial TiO 2 (Degussa P25), and the KNb 3 O 8 and HNb 3 O 8 particles prepared by conventional solid state reaction.It was also found that either the HNb 3 O 8 nanobelts or the HNb 3 O 8 particles performed better than the corresponding KNb 3 O 8 counterpart.It is proposed that the nanobelt-like morphology and the protonic acidity contribute to the higher photocatalytic activity of the HNb 3 O 8 nanobelts.

title: Experimental
content: The KNb 3 O 8 samples were prepared by the solid state reaction (SSR) and the hydrothermal (HT) synthesis, respectively.In the solid state reaction case, stoichiometric amounts of Nb 2 O 5 and K 2 CO 3 were well milled and then heated at 900 C for 10 h; the obtained product was denoted as KNb 3 O 8 (SSR).In the case of the hydrothermal synthesis, Nb 2 O 5 (0.5 g) powder was first dispersed 0926-860X -see front matter 2011 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.doi:10.1016j.apcata.2011.10.044 in KOH aqueous solution (40 ml, 2 mol L -1 ) for half an hour, the slurry was loaded into a Teflon-lined stainless autoclave and was then treated at 180 C for 2 days to give a clear solution.The pH value of the solution was adjusted to 5.5 and 2.0 g of P123 (Aldrich, average molecular weight: 5800) was added; the reaction mixture was further treated at 180 C for 4 days.The white precipitate was recovered by filtration, washed thoroughly with ethanol and water, dried at 70 C for 12 h, and then heated at 500 C for 1 h.The product so obtained was denoted as KNb 3 O 8 (HT).The HNb 3 O 8 solid acid samples were obtained by stirring the corresponding potassium salts in aqueous nitric acid solution (5 mol L -1 , 60 ml per gram of KNb 3 O 8 ) at room temperature for 2 days.The HNb 3 O 8 samples were denoted as HNb 3 O 8 (SSR) and HNb 3 O 8 (HT), respectively. The phase compositions of samples were identified by X-Ray Powder Diffraction (Cu K radiation, Bruker AXS-D8) in the 2A range of 3-90 .The UV-vis diffuse reflectance spectra were recorded at room temperature on a Shimadzu UV-2450 UV-vis spectrometer with barium sulfate as the reference sample.Specific surface areas of samples were deduced by the BET method (N 2 adsorption) with a NOVA-2000E instrument.FT-IR spectra of the samples were collected on a Nicolet Nexus 470 FT-IR spectrophotometer at room temperature by KBr method.Morphologies of samples were characterized using a scanning electron microscope (JSM-7001F, JEOL). The photocatalytic reduction of CO 2 with gaseous H 2 O was carried out in a quartz tubular reactor (length: 28.0 cm, 3.0 cm; volume: 159 ml) that was placed horizontally (Scheme 1).A flat quartz plate was used to hold the catalyst (typically 0.1 g).A 350 W Xe-lamp (Nanshen Company, Shanghai) was used as light source.Before the photocatalytic reaction, high purity CO 2 (99.995) was bubbled through deionized water and then flowed through the reactor for more than 1 h to ensure that air was eliminated.The reactor was then sealed and the light was turned on to start the reaction.The volume ratio of water vapor to CO 2 in the feed was 0.08:1.The internal temperature of the reactor was controlled at 45 2 C. The products in the gas phase were analyzed with a gas chromatograph system (GC-9790A), using a flame ionization detector (FID) for organic compounds determination and a thermal conductivity detector (TCD) for inorganic gas determination.The carbon balance exceeded 99.1).From Fig. 1B one can see that the 020 diffraction peaks of the acid samples shift ca.0.5 to the smaller angle end compared with that of the potassium salts.The d 0 2 0 values of the acid samples increased ca.0.7 A accordingly, and this is ascribed to the intercalation of water molecules at the interlayer space through hydrogen bonding 19.Fig. 3 shows the FT-IR spectra of HNb 3 O 8 and KNb 3 O 8 samples.The spectra of the samples are consistent with those reported in the literature and the IR absorption in the range of 400-1000 cm -1 is assigned to the vibration of the Nb 3 O 8 -host slice 22.Compared with the samples prepared by the solid state reaction, samples prepared by hydrothermal synthesis show weaker IR absorption and this is probably because of the lower crystallinity.by the hydrothermal synthesis exhibited quite distinct morphologies.Fig. 4C andD shows clearly that the KNb 3 O 8 (HT) sample contains thin nanobelts that are several micrometers in length, 300-500 nm in width, and ca.50 nm in thickness.After proton exchange, the nanobelt-like morphology was well retained for the HNb 3 O 8 (HT) sample (Fig. 4E).As a consequence of the change in sample morphology, the surface area value of the samples changed notably.From Table 1 one can see that the surface area value increased from 2.7 m 2 g -1 of KNb 3 O 8 (SSR) to 28.8 m 2 g -1 of KNb 3 O 8 (HT), and from 6.5 m 2 g -1 of HNb 3 O 8 (SSR) to 39.4 m 2 g -1 of HNb 3 O 8 (HT).In a previous report on the preparation of KNb 3 O 8 by a similar hydrothermal synthesis yet no P123 was added, the obtained KNb 3 O 8 sample was leaf-like and the surface area value was only 7.2 m 2 g -1 23.It is likely that P123 adopted in the present study could serve as liquid template and assisted the growth of KNb 3 O 8 nanobelts.

title: Results and discussion
content: Fig. 5 shows the UV-vis absorption spectra of samples.All the samples absorb light in the range of 350 nm.The absorption edges of the samples prepared by hydrothermal synthesis shift slightly to the shorter wavelength end.The bandgap values were estimated from the sharp absorption edges of the spectra, and the data are listed in Table 1. Fig. 6 shows the yield of methane over the KNb 3 O 8 and HNb 3 O 8 samples as a function of irradiation time.For all the samples the amount of detected methane increased almost linearly with irradiation time.In contrast, there was no product detected under the dark condition or under UV irradiation without any photocatalyst, indicating that the present reaction of CO 2 reduction with H 2 O is indeed a photocatalytic process.Methane is the only one hydrocarbon product detected in the gas phase.The samples after CO 2 photoreduction were checked by FT-IR analysis, and there was no adsorbed organic species observed on sample surface.There had been reports that methanol was formed over zeolite supported TiO 2 photocatalysts 24,25 and ethanol was formed over a BiVO 4 photocatalyst 3 in CO 2 photoreduction.It is likely that the selectivity of CO 2 photoreduction varies with the type of photocatalyst and the reaction conditions.The samples prepared by hydrothermal synthesis exhibited much higher activities than the samples prepared by solid state reaction.As listed in Table HT and the SSR cases, the solid acid sample exhibited higher activity than the corresponding potassium salt.For example, the rate of CH 4 formation over HNb 3 O 8 (HT) reaches 3.58 mol g cat. -1 h -1 , which is two times that (1.71 mol g cat. -1 h -1 ) achieved over KNb 3 O 8 (HT). For a comparison study, commercial TiO 2 (Degussa P25) was also tested under identical conditions.From Table 1 one can see that the rates of methane formation over KNb 3 O 8 (HT) and HNb 3 O 8 (HT) are much higher than that (0.37 mol g cat. -1 h -1 ) observed over In the photoreduction of CO 2 by gaseous water, the photocatalyst was excited by the irradiation light to generate electrons (e -) at the conduction band and holes (h ) at the valence band.The adsorbed H 2 O molecules reacted with h to form O 2 and H , H then reacted with e -to form H radicals.Subsequently, CO 2 was reduced by H to hydrocarbons 25262728.Catalysts with larger surface area values usually could provide more reaction active sites.In the present study, the samples prepared by the hydrothermal synthesis possess larger surface area values than the counterparts prepared by the solid state reaction.Interestingly, compared with the enhancement in surface area value, greater times enhancement in the activity was observed for both KNb 3 O 8 and HNb 3 O 8 prepared by hydrothermal synthesis (Fig. 9).Taking the HNb 3 O 8 (HT) sample as an example, the surface area value increased six times, but the activity increased more than twenty times.Moreover, as mentioned above, in either of the HT and SSR cases, the solid acid sample exhibited higher activity than the corresponding potassium The adsorption of water should also have significant influence in the present heterogeneous photocatalytic reaction.As illustrated in Fig. 10, water molecules could be easily intercalated into the interlayer space of the solid acid samples through hydrogen bonding 171819; this facilitates the trapping of photogenerated holes and the formation of H species, and consequently leads to the higher rates of methane formation over the acid samples.Because CO 2 is an acidic gas in nature, it might show weak adsorption on the surface of HNb that of CO 2 .In summary, the novel nanobelt-like morphology and the stronger adsorption ability to water molecules contribute to the overall higher photocatalytic activity of HNb 3 O 8 (HT). There was no O 2 detected in the photocatalytic reduction of CO 2 over the HNb 3 O 8 and KNb 3 O 8 samples in the current study.We consider that this phenomenon might be attributed to the behavior of holes on the surface of the photocatalysts.Similar observation was also made in some other photocatalytic processes.There have bee reports that in the cases of pure water splitting over InMO 4 (M V, Nb, and Ta) 293031, Bi 2 InNbO 7 32, and MIn 0.5 Nb 0.5 O 3 (M Ca, Sr, and Ba) 33, there was only H 2 detected while no O 2 detected.In the case of TiO 2 photocatalysis, the produced O 2 can be photoadsorbed on the surface of the photocatalyst under UV irradiation; the oxygen molecules on TiO 2 surface could be physisorbed and chemsorbed 34,35.The physisorbed O 2 molecules are produced through the neutralization of chemisorbed O 2 -species by photogenerated holes 36.We suppose that in the present study the produced O 2 molecules were photoadsorbed on the surface of the photocatalysts in a physisorbed andor a chemisorbed state.In the present study, molecular hydrogen was also not detected in the photocatalytic reduction of CO 2 .Because the concentration of CO 2 in the reactor is much higher than that of photogenerated H radicals, we consider that the probability of H radicals being trapped by CO 2 to form hydrocarbon is larger than the probability of two H radicals react to form H 2 .Although molecular hydrogen was not detected, we cannot exclude that water splitting takes place in the present study.Since H 2 can also serve as reductant for CO 2 photoreduction 37, the trace amount of photogenerated H 2 (if there is any) could be quickly consumed by CO 2 and therefore could hardly be detected.

title: Conclusions
content: KNb 3 O 8 and HNb 3 O 8 samples with different morphologies, viz.micron-sized particles and nanobelts, were successfully prepared by the SSR and the HT synthesis, respectively.The SEM images show that the KNb 3 O 8 and HNb 3 O 8 nanobelts are several micrometers in length, 300-500 nm in width, and ca.50 nm in thickness.Compared with the corresponding particle samples, the KNb 3 O 8 and HNb 3 O 8 nanobelts exhibited seventeen and twenty times higher activities for CO 2 photoreduction to methane, respectively.In either of the HT and SSR cases, the HNb 3 O 8 solid acid sample exhibited higher activity than the KNb 3 O 8 potassium salt.The rate of CH 4 formation over HNb 3 O 8 (HT) reaches 3.58 mol g cat. -1 h -1 , the data is two times that (1.71 mol g cat. -1 h -1 ) achieved over KNb 3 O 8 (HT).It is proposed that the nanobelt-like morphology and the intercalation of water molecules through hydrogen bonding facilitates the separation and trapping of photogenerated carriers at the interlayer surface of HNb 3 O 8 (HT), and this contributes notably to the higher photocatalytic activity for CO 2 photoreduction. Fig. 1A shows the XRD patterns of KNb 3 O 8 and HNb 3 O 8 samples prepared by different methods.All the samples were crystallized in pure phase, and their patterns match well with the published JCPDS files (files no.: 01-075-2182 for KNb 3 O 8 and 00-044-0672 for HNb 3 O 8 ).The diffraction intensities observed for the samples prepared by hydrothermal synthesis are weaker than those for samples prepared by solid state reaction; this is probably owing to the decrease in crystallinity and the change in dimension of the samples.HNb 3 O 8 is isostructural with KNb 3 O 8 , and both samples crystallize in an orthorhombic symmetry 19.As depicted by Fig. 2, HNb 3 O 8 is constructed of 2-dimensional Nb 3 O 8 -anion slices built by corner-and edge-sharing NbO 6 octahedra, and the H cations are located between the slices 19,20.The 0 2 0 diffraction peaks are characteristic of the layered structures of samples 21,22, Fig. 2. The schematic drawing of the layered structure of HNb3O8. Fig. 3. FT-IR spectra of (a) KNb3O8(HT); (b) HNb3O8(HT); (c) KNb3O8(SSR); (d) HNb3O8(SSR). Fig. 4. SEM images of (A): KNb3O8(SSR); (B): HNb3O8(SSR); (C and D): KNb3O8(HT); (E): HNb3O8(HT). Fig. 5. Absorption spectra of (a): KNb3O8(HT); (b): HNb3O8(HT); (c): KNb3O8(SSR); (d): HNb3O8(SSR). Fig. 6.The rate of CH4 formation over (a): HNb3O8(HT); (b): KNb3O8(HT); (c): HNb3O8(SSR); (d): KNb3O8(SSR).Reaction conditions: catalyst: 0.1 g; H2O: CO2 0.08:1; reaction temperature: 45 2 C. The inset shows the conversion of CO2 over the HNb3O8(HT) nanobelts. Fig. 7.The rate of CH4 formation over HNb3O8(HT) nanobelts derived from a KNb3O8(HT) nanobelt sample that has been calcined at 700 C for 4 h in air.Reaction conditions: catalyst: 0.1 g; H2O: CO2 0.08:1; reaction temperature: 45 2 C. Fig. 8. Repeated reactions of CO2 photocatalytic reduction over HNb3O8(HT) nanobelts.Reaction conditions: catalyst: 0.1 g; H2O: CO2 0.08:1; reaction temperature: 45 2 C. Fig.9.The enhancement in surface area value and photocatalytic activity for KNb3O8 and HNb3O8 samples prepared by different methods. Fig. 10.The proposed mechanism of CO2 photoreduction over the HNb3O8 nanobelt. The physical characteristics of samples and the rate of methane formation. 1, the rate of CH 4 formation over KNb 3 O 8 (HT) is seventeen times that over KNb 3 O 8 (SSR), and the rate of CH 4 formation over HNb 3 O 8 (HT) is about twenty times that over HNb 3 O 8 (SSR).It was also noted that in either of the This research was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (no.21003064), the Research Foundation of Jiangsu University (no.12830003723), and the Scientific Research Foundation for the Returned Overseas Chinese Scholars, State Education Ministry.
